emails.subject: Re: Recommendations for Japanese Electricity Reform
emails.body: Re: Recommendations for Japanese Electricity Reform Some thoughts 1. We argue against the PJM Installed Capacity obligation. Basically it is another rent capture system for generators. 2. Don't really understand your stranded cost recovery scheme. Looks like a transmission level charge. Is it $/mw or $/mwh? 3. Your model for a pool doesn't solve the key problem of market power. Putting a pool in place w/o dealing w/ market power is a recipe for disaster. 4. You don't talk about "Market Based Rate" authority. Would all generators, including incumbent generation be allowed to bid their price or must they bid cost? This is critical. This is the real carrot to get the Utilities off of the stick mandatory participation with cost-based bidding for Incumbents until under 1,000 HHI. Good luck, Jim Mark Crowther 04/30/2001 11:56 PM

emails.subject: Re: Recommendations for Japanese Electricity Reform
emails.body: Jim, Thanks for taking time to look through the Brattle Group recommendations for Japan. Some of your comments refer to serious issues in the design of a competitive market for electricity here. Brattle's analysis of the Japanese electricity system concludes that market concentration is the main reason for the failure of current reforms. The recommendations then focus on providing practical means of reducing market power in Japan, followed by implementation of a pool with bilateral trading to create non-discriminatory access to network resources and services. Would you be able to take some time to talk to Nick O'Day and myself about your comments on Tuesday evening? Around 5.30 p.m. your time would be ideal for us if you're available. Best regards Mark James D Steffes 05/05/2001 09:30 AM
